main_sections.title: The Day the River Spoke
main_sections.prose_summary: The story is set in a coastal village in India and centres on Jahnavi, a bright girl of nearly ten who has been denied schooling while her brothers Gopi (Ettan) and Ramu attend school. Her days are spent minding the youngest sibling, little Appu. One afternoon, weeping by the river, Jahnavi is astonished to hear the River speaking to her in a sleepy, murmuring voice. The River, characterised as wise, affectionate, and gently humorous, listens patiently to Jahnavi's longing — she wants to read like her brothers, and to understand the mysteries of the natural world: why spiders are yellow in yellow flowers, why bamboo rustles, why tadpoles become frogs, why the moon always rises from behind the hills. The River dispels her self-doubt with quiet confidence, pointing out that little girls swim as fast as little boys and can do as much. It advises her simply: slip into school one morning, sit down, and listen. Jahnavi protests her fear of being chased away, but the River reminds her she is unafraid of lizards and snakes and rattling trains. The story also weaves in images of ships and the catamaran of Chandu the fisherman, widening Jahnavi's sense of a larger world. Emboldened, Jahnavi creeps into the classroom the next day with little Appu asleep on her shoulder and is noticed by the kind teacher, who visits the family that evening. Her father, seen anxiously scratching his cheek, gives way; her mother movingly recalls her own denied schooling and encourages Jahnavi. The story closes with Jahnavi running to the river at dawn to announce her triumph, and the River responding with a sleepy chuckle and a promise to tell her about the ships that sail the sea. The central theme is the right of every child, especially girls, to education — a theme enacted through courage, community, and the unexpected wisdom of nature itself.

reading.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect
tasks_verbatim: Close your eyes and quietly listen to the sounds around you for a minute. Are they trying to tell you something? Do you think the things around you also listen to you all day long? Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

Jahnavi was a bright young girl who lived with her parents and three brothers in a coastal village in India. Her parents worked on the farm. She had a dream. What could that dream be? How could she make her dream come true? What is your dream? What can you do to make your dream come true? Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.
Complete the table given below with Jahnavi's questions in Column 1 and the River's answers in Column 2. Check your answers with the teacher.

Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'You shouldn't cry, you know...' (i) Complete the sentence with the correct option — tone of the River is (assuring/sympathising). (ii) Select a phrase from the extract which shows that Jahnavi was a frequent visitor to the spot. (iii) Choose the correct option: The use of an exclamation mark at the end of 'It couldn't be the river!' expresses — A. excitement B. hesitation C. irritation D. disbelief. (iv) Choose three qualities of the River highlighted in the extract from the words: affectionate, forgiving, thoughtful, impatient, kind-hearted.

Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. Extract 2: 'Can I do something?...' (i) Complete the sentence: According to the River, there was no difference between girls and boys because ____. (ii) Identify whether the following statement is true or false: The River encourages Jahnavi to have faith in herself. (iii) Choose the correct option: When the River suggests Jahnavi should 'slip along', it means she should move — A. quickly B. casually C. quietly D. confidently. (iv) What does the repetition of the phrase 'I couldn't' tell us?

Answer the following questions: (1) Why does the writer describe different aspects of nature in great detail at the beginning? (2) How did the River know so much about Jahnavi? (3) What can you infer about Jahnavi and the school from 'And I'm so old now, they'll never let me go'? (4) Why did the River laugh when Jahnavi said she would be scared at school? (5) Why should Jahnavi follow the River's advice? (6) How does the conversation between Jahnavi and the River make the story more appealing? (7) What is the main message that the writer intends to convey?

question_bank.stem: Complete the table given below with Jahnavi's questions in Column 1 and the River's answers in Column 2.
question_bank.table:
Column 1|Column 2
...why the moon always comes from behind the hills…?|[blank]
[blank]|… little girls can do as much as little boys.
[blank]|Big boats, so big that they can take hundreds of people, and they sail along the sea with lights that shine all night.
Will they come here?|[blank]

The River encourages Jahnavi to have faith in herself and fulfill her desire to go to school. (True/False)

tasks_verbatim: You have learnt that naming words are called nouns and only proper nouns begin with a capital letter. Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

There are a few words related to sound in the text. Pick the words from the text and write them next to the correct definition (shrieked, splashed, murmuring, wailed, rustle, gasped, rattling, noisy/loud, wailed). Now, fill in the blanks with suitable sound words from exercise (II).

The word 'catamaran' is used in the text. The word originates from Tamil language 'kattu maram' meaning 'tied wood'. Find out the meanings of these words and from which Indian language they were borrowed: karma, sahib, chutney, guru, verandah, jungle, areca, palanquin.

Chandu the fisherman has a catamaran. Given below are some boats used in different parts of India: canoe, catamaran, coracle, shikara. (i) Which one would you like to travel in? (ii) Make a colourful drawing of a boat that you would like to travel in.
Solve the crossword puzzle based on words related to water transport (ship, boat, raft, steamer, canoe, tugboat, yacht).

Read the highlighted words in the sentence. Prepositions are words that indicate the relationship between nouns or pronouns and other elements in a sentence. They show direction, location, time or logical relationships between ideas. Fill in the blanks with suitable prepositions given within the brackets.
Words like on, off, up, down can function both as preposition and as adverb. Fill in the blanks with prepositions or adverbs.

question_bank.stem: Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

Pick the words from the text related to sound and write them next to the correct definition: (1) made a high-pitched piercing sound (2) to cause liquid to strike or fall on something (3) a low continuous background noise (4) cry with loud uncontrollable gasps (5) to make a soft dry sound, like paper or leaves moving (6) took a short quick breath through the mouth due to surprise, pain or shock (7) shaking rapidly to make continuous short, sharp sounds (8) making a lot of noise (9) made a long, high cry, usually because of pain or sadness.

Fill in the blanks with suitable sound words: The classroom grew (i) _______ as the students noticed the teacher carrying the answer papers. As the teacher turned the pages with a (ii) _______, the children (iii) _______, wondering what comments awaited. Someone (iv) _______ in frustration, and the (v) _______ of the students steadily increased. Finally, when the teacher praised the students, the students (vi) _______ with excitement.
Find out the meanings of these words and from which Indian language they were borrowed: karma (Sanskrit), sahib, chutney, guru, verandah, jungle, areca, palanquin.

Given below are some boats used in different parts of India: canoe, catamaran, coracle, shikara. (i) Which one would you like to travel in? (ii) Make a colourful drawing of a boat that you would like to travel in.

Solve the crossword puzzle based on words related to water transport. Across: 1. a large vessel for transporting goods or passengers by sea; 2. a small vessel for travelling on water typically propelled by oars, sails, or an engine; 3. a flat floating structure for travelling across water; 4. a boat or ship driven by steam. Down: 5. a small, light, narrow boat pointed at both ends moved using a paddle; 6. a boat with a powerful engine used to pull large ships; 7. a boat with sails and sometimes an engine used for racing or travelling, rhymes with 'cot'.

Fill in the blanks with suitable prepositions: Jahnavi takes the River's advice and goes to school. On her first day 1. _________ (at/in/on) school, Jahnavi stood nervously 2. _________ (in/outside/above) the gate, clutching her bag. The bell rang and she hurried 3. _________ (outside/through/inside) the bustling classroom. She found a seat 4. _________ (among/between/from) two friendly classmates. The teacher started the lesson 5. _________ (about/in/for) numbers. Jahnavi listened attentively, feeling excited 6. _________ (at/about/for) the new adventure ahead.

Fill in the blanks with prepositions or adverbs: 1. The cat jumped ___________ the table. (preposition) 2. The children played ___________ until dark. (adverb) 3. She quickly ran ___________ the park. (preposition) 4. The helicopter was hovering ___________. (adverb) 5. We were just walking ___________, chatting. (adverb) 6. After thoroughly wiping his shoes, he stepped ___________. (adverb) 7. She drove slowly ___________ the sharp curve. (preposition) 8. He placed the keys ___________ the mat before leaving. (preposition)

tasks_verbatim: Read the words given in the box below. You will listen to five people speak about school life. As you listen, circle the words that are used by the speakers. There are two words you do not need. (lawyer, newspapers, marriage, desks, homemaker, advertisements, principal, careers, posters, books)
You will once again listen to the five speakers. As you listen, match each statement 1–7 to each speaker (i)–(v). There are two statements that you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Read the words in the box. Listen to five people speak about school life. Circle the words used by the speakers. (There are two words you do not need.) Words: lawyer, newspapers, marriage, desks, homemaker, advertisements, principal, careers, posters, books.

Listen to the five speakers again. Match each statement 1–7 to each speaker (i)–(v). There are two statements you do not need. Statements: 1. Promotes active participation for all in school activities. 2. Comments on how school makes learning Mathematics joyful. 3. Believes in the power of education that leads to freedom of choice. 4. Shares that school also means companionship. 5. Understands that boys and girls have different abilities. 6. Persuades the parent to grant permission to attend school. 7. Believes that learning skills is not gender-based.

tasks_verbatim: The River spoke to Jahnavi in a 'sleepy' voice. Work in pairs and take turns to speak the same sentence in 'other' voices. Some hints are given below: happy, surprised, angry, scared, sad, worried.

Jahnavi asks the River for advice and the River gives advice. Read the given situations and work in pairs to ask for and give advice. Take turns to change your roles. Use the prompts given. Situations: (i) You wish to play football at school but it has an all-boys' team only. (ii) You have been unable to score well in Mathematics due to nervousness during exams. (iii) You really enjoy music classes but do not get enough time to practise. (iv) You cannot see the blackboard clearly from the last bench.

question_bank.stem: Work in pairs and take turns to speak the sentence 'Dear, dear! What's the matter?...' in different voices: happy, surprised, angry, scared, sad, worried.

Work in pairs to ask for and give advice for the situations: (i) You wish to play football but the school has an all-boys' team only. (ii) You have been unable to score well in Mathematics due to nervousness during exams. (iii) You enjoy music classes but do not get enough time to practise. (iv) You cannot see the blackboard clearly from the last bench. Use the given prompts for asking and giving advice.

tasks_verbatim: Take some time to observe the world around you. Over the next few days, pay attention to the colours, shapes, patterns, and textures found in nature. Make detailed notes of what you observe. Now, write a descriptive paragraph based on what you've observed. Highlight the colours, shapes, textures, and unique details that made an impression on you. Give a title to your paragraph.

question_bank.stem: Observe the natural world around you over the next few days — colours, shapes, patterns, textures of plants, trees, flowers, birds, the sky. Record your observations. Then write a descriptive paragraph based on what you've observed. Give a title to your paragraph.

tasks_verbatim: Discuss why rivers in India are considered to be sacred and most of them are referred to as female. Some rivers are referred to as male, for example, the Brahmaputra and the Sone.

Whole Class Activity: (1) India is a multilingual country, and many of us can speak two to three languages easily. Make a list of the languages that everyone in the class can speak and display it on a chart paper in the classroom. (2) In which language do you enjoy reading and learning the most?

Read the poster given below and answer the questions: (1) What are the things you can observe in the poster? (2) Why do you think the girls look happy? (3) Dignity means 'self-respect'. What is the relation between 'dignity' and 'happiness'? (4) How does a Nation prosper when its daughters are happy?

The Government has launched many schemes for the education of the girl child. Some of them are 'Beti Bachao Beti Padhao', 'Balika Samridhi Yojana', 'Samagra Shiksha Scheme – Kasturba Gandhi Balika Vidyalayas', etc. Find out more such schemes from the internet or from your teacher and spread awareness in your neighbourhood.

main_sections.title: Try Again
main_sections.poem_text:
King Bruce of Scotland flung himself down
In a lonely mood to think;
'Tis true he was monarch, and wore a crown,
But his heart was beginning to sink.

For he had been trying to do a great deed,
To make his people glad;
He had tried and tried, but couldn't succeed
And so he became quite sad.

He flung himself down in low despair,
As grieved as man could be;
And after a while he pondered there,
"I'll give it all up," said he.

Now just at that moment a spider dropped,
With its silken, filmy clue;
And the King, in the midst of his thinking, stopped
To see what the spider would do.

'Twas a long way up to the ceiling dome,
And it hung by a rope so fine;
That how it would get to its cobweb home,
King Bruce could not divine.

It soon began to cling and crawl
Straight up with strong endeavour;
But down it came with a slippery sprawl,
As near to the ground as ever.

Up, up it ran, not a second to stay,
To utter the least complaint;
Till it fell still lower, and there it lay,
A little dizzy and faint.

Its head grew steady — again it went,
And travelled a half-yard higher;
'Twas a delicate thread it had to tread,
And a road where its feet would tire.

Again it fell and swung below,
But again it quickly mounted;
Till up and down, now fast, now slow,
Nine brave attempts were counted.

"Sure," cried the King, "that foolish thing
Will strive no more to climb;
When it toils so hard to reach and cling,
And tumbles every time."

But up the insect went once more,
Ah me! 'tis an anxious minute;
He's only a foot from his cobweb door,
Oh say, will he lose or win it?

Steadily, steadily, inch by inch,
Higher and higher he got;
And a bold little run at the very last pinch
Put him into his native cot.

"Bravo, bravo!" the King cried out,
"All honour to those who try;
The spider up there, defied despair;
He conquered, and why shouldn't I?"

And Bruce of Scotland braced his mind,
And gossips tell the tale,
That he tried once more as he tried before,
And that time did not fail.

— Eliza Cook
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Written in a ballad-like narrative style with an ABAB rhyme scheme, 'Try Again' by Eliza Cook recounts the story of King Bruce of Scotland, who, despairing of repeated failure in a great cause, is transformed by watching a spider attempt to climb to its cobweb home nine times before succeeding. The poem's central theme is perseverance — the idea that success comes to those who refuse to surrender despite failure. The tone shifts from melancholy and defeat in the opening stanzas to mounting tension as the spider's final climb is described with breathless suspense ('Ah me! 'tis an anxious minute'), before arriving at triumphant resolution when the King takes inspiration from the insect and resolves to try once more. The dominant imagery is of upward struggle against gravity — 'up, up it ran', 'steadily, steadily, inch by inch', 'higher and higher' — mirroring the psychological journey from despair to renewed determination. Alliteration ('silken, filmy'; 'delicate thread it had to tread'), repetition ('tried and tried'; 'steadily, steadily'), and the device of metaphor (the spider's climb as an implied comparison to the King's own struggle for his people) enrich the poem's didactic force.

reading.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect
tasks_verbatim: Work in pairs, identify what you see in the pictures given below. What can we learn from them? Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.
Complete the summary of the poem by filling in the blanks with suitable words from the text given in the box. Words: tried, conquered, mounted, low, despair, strive, attempts, great.
Complete the following sentences suitably: (1) We can say that the poet uses the narrative style because ________. (2) The central idea of the poem is ________.

Pick three examples from the poem for each of the following: (1) lines that describe the spider's efforts (2) alliteration (the repetition of the same consonant sounds at the beginning of closely connected words).
Why does the poet repeat the following words or phrases in the poem? tried and tried; steadily, steadily; up, up.

Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct answer from within the brackets: (1) The rhyme scheme of the poem is ____________ (AABB/ABBA/ABAB). (2) 'Bravo' is an example of ____________ (conjunction/interjection/adjective). (3) The poet uses 'twas and 'tis for the sake of ____________ (rhythm/rhyme/contraction).
The spider's climbing is compared to King Bruce's own struggles. This poetic device is called metaphor. Read the sentences and identify metaphors and similes, write M for metaphor and S for simile.
Read the following sentences and transform the similes to metaphors and metaphors to similes.

Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'He flung himself down in low despair... And after a while he pondered there... Now just at that moment a spider dropped...' (i) Select the phrase which shows the physical expression of the King's emotional state. (ii) Why does the poet use the word 'low' before despair? (iii) Choose the correct option: 'pondered' means — A. paused B. thought C. noticed D. rested. (iv) The phrase 'silken filmy clue' creates an image of something that is (long and light / very delicate and soft).

Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. Extract 2: '...when it toils so hard to reach and cling... But up the insect went once more...' (i) Choose the correct option to complete the analogy: toil: hard :: ___ : ___. (ii) List any two characteristics of the spider highlighted in 'But up the insect went once more'. (iii) The expression 'Ah me!' indicates — A. happiness B. stress C. relief D. loneliness. (iv) Complete: Readers are able to relate to this extract because ____.

Answer the following questions: (1) How does the first stanza help in setting the mood of the poem? (2) Describe how King Bruce's attitude changes from beginning to end. (3) The poet describes every action of the spider in great detail. What does this tell us about his attitude towards the spider? (4) How does the spider inspire us to overcome despair and not give up? (5) The poem teaches us that failures are stepping stones to success. Explain. (6) The spider's journey tells us that anyone can be a source of inspiration. Elaborate.

The poem tells the story of King Bruce of Scotland, who was feeling 1. _____ after failing multiple times to achieve something 2. _____ for his people. In a moment of 3. _____, he observed a spider trying to reach its web. The spider 4. _____ every time it fell after getting close. It made nine 5. _____ and finally succeeded. The king said the spider had 6. _____ and so would he. Inspired by the spider's determination, King Bruce decided to 7. _____. When he 8. _____ this time, he ultimately succeeded.

Read the following sentences and identify metaphors and similes. Write M for metaphor and S for simile. (i) He ran as fast as a cheetah. (ii) The world is a stage. (iii) The night was as dark as coal. (iv) He sang like an angel. (v) Her voice was music to his ears. (vi) The classroom was a zoo. (vii) The baby slept like a log. (viii) Her smile was as bright as the sun. (ix) The lake was a mirror, reflecting the sky.

Read the following sentences and transform the similes to metaphors and metaphors to similes. (iii) The book was a treasure chest, filled with endless adventures. (iv) Her laughter was a melody that brightened the darkest days. (v) The mountain stood as a guard, watching over the valley below. (vi) The night was like a velvet cloak, wrapping the world in mystery. (vii) Her thoughts were like butterflies in a summer meadow.

Select the phrase which shows the physical expression of the King's emotional state from: 'He flung himself down in low despair, / As grieved as man could be; / And after a while he pondered there, / "I'll give it all up," said he.'
Why does the poet use the word 'low' before despair?
In the line 'And after a while he pondered there', the word 'pondered' means _____.
The phrase 'silken filmy clue' creates an image of something that is _____ (long and light / very delicate and soft).
Choose the correct option to complete the analogy: toil: hard :: ___: ___.
List any two characteristics of the spider highlighted in the line 'But up the insect went once more'.
The expression 'Ah me!' in the extract indicates a sense of _____.
Readers are able to relate to this extract because ____.
How does the first stanza help in setting the mood of the poem?
Describe how King Bruce's attitude changes from the beginning towards the end of the poem.
The poet describes every action of the spider in great detail. What does this tell us about his attitude towards the spider?
How does the spider inspire us to overcome despair and not give up?
The poem teaches us that failures are stepping stones to success. Explain.
The spider's journey tells us that anyone can be a source of inspiration in our lives. Elaborate.

tasks_verbatim: Write the opposites of the following words taken from the poem: up, fast, glad, win, succeed.

Choose the correct meaning of the underlined words in the sentences from the box given below. Frame sentences of your own for these words: (1) work very hard and/or for a long time (2) prepared themselves for something difficult (3) an attempt to do something new or difficult (4) to try very hard to achieve something. Sentences: (i) Please make every endeavour to arrive on time. (ii) Ants toil tirelessly to save food for rainy season. (iii) We encourage all students to strive to do their best. (iv) The sailors braced themselves against the strong wind.

The poem uses words that show distance (a long way up, inch by inch, higher and higher, half-yard higher). Classify the words or phrases in the box based on the distance (far or near): proximity, remoteness, light year, middle of nowhere, yonder, hairline, vicinity, afar, farther, adjacent, a stone's throw.
Read the following line from the poem: 'Twas a delicate thread it had to tread. Repeat the line five to six times. Are you able to say it clearly? Create more tongue twisters of your own.

(ii) Ants toil tirelessly to save food for rainy season. (iii) We encourage all students to strive to do their best. (iv) The sailors braced themselves against the strong wind. tasks_verbatim: You will listen to a story about an ant. Read the questions given below and before you listen, guess the answers to these questions.
Now, listen to the story once again and as you listen, check whether your answers are correct.

question_bank.stem: Listen to a story about an ant. Before listening, guess the answers: (1) What was the ant known for? (i) determination (ii) strength (iii) wisdom. (2) Why was the ant unable to take the roti home? (i) It was stuck under a rock. (ii) It was too heavy to carry. (iii) It was pulled by other ants. (3) What was the bird doing? (i) playing with the ant (ii) searching for food (iii) picking up leaves. (4) How did the bird make the ant feel? (i) proud (ii) inspired (iii) relaxed. (5) How did the fellow ants help? (i) by advising the ant continuously (ii) by standing still near the ant (iii) by joining the ant in her efforts.

tasks_verbatim: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Your perseverance helped you. Use the following sentence prompts as a guide for your narration.

question_bank.stem: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Use the given sentence prompts as a guide: Introduction (I remember a time when I felt like giving up...), Describe the challenge, Feeling disheartened, The source of motivation, Taking action, The result, Reflection.

tasks_verbatim: King Bruce was inspired by the spider that kept on trying till it was successful. Think about the steps you can take to overcome a difficult situation and write them down. Now, write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Also, advise not to quit and persevere. Remember to use transition words like 'To begin with…', 'Next…', 'After that…', 'Then...', 'Finally...' to share the steps of your plan.

question_bank.stem: Think about the steps you can take to overcome a difficult situation. Write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation, advising not to quit and persevere. Use transition words like 'To begin with…', 'Next…', 'After that…', 'Then...', 'Finally...'.

tasks_verbatim: Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.

Did you know that spiders are not insects? They belong to a group called the arachnids. Observe a spider and an ant from a distance and note down the differences between them. Share your observations with your classmates. Ask your Science teacher for more information.

Let us learn how to weave a spider web using three sticks and a roll of old wool (instructions in four figures). Decorate it with words and phrases you learnt about the spider in the poem or anything else you like.

question_bank.stem: Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.
Observe a spider and an ant from a distance. Note down the differences between them. Share your observations with your classmates. Ask your Science teacher for more information about spiders.
Learn how to weave a spider web using three sticks and a roll of old wool (following the four-step figure instructions). Decorate it with words and phrases from the poem.

main_sections.title: Three Days to See
main_sections.prose_summary: This extract is written by Helen Keller, who has been blind since early childhood. The piece opens with Keller describing the richness of her tactile experience — the delicate symmetry of a leaf, the smooth skin of a silver birch, the rough bark of a pine, the quiver of a bird felt through a branch in spring — asserting that even without sight she finds hundreds of things to interest her. From this richly sensory opening she pivots to a longing: if she were given sight for three days, how would she spend them? She divides this imagined gift into three parts. On the first day she would look at the faces of those whose kindness has made her life worth living, seeing into the heart of a friend through the 'window of the soul' — the eye. On the second day she would arise with the dawn, witness the miracle of night becoming day, and then spend the day at museums seeing the condensed history of the earth — dinosaurs, mastodons, the pageant of human progress. On the third day she would go to the city, stand at a busy street corner, and simply watch people going about their daily lives — seeing their smiles, determination, suffering. At midnight permanent night would descend again. The piece closes with a universally applicable exhortation: use your eyes as if you would be blinded tomorrow; listen to music, bird-song, and the human voice as if you would be deaf tomorrow; touch, smell, and taste with full awareness as if those senses could fail. The central theme is appreciation of the gifts of sense that most people take for granted, and the dignity and richness of human experience however constrained. The tone is reflective, eloquent, and gently instructive.

reading.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect
tasks_verbatim: Work in pairs. Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details and write about: (1) Name of the place (2) The person(s) you went with (3) The things that you could see, listen, smell, taste, touch and feel.
How does this memory make you feel?
Do you think your visit/experience would be different if you could not see or hear? Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

Work in pairs. Identify the statements that are true from the ones given below. Share your answers with the teacher: (1) The author expresses a deep longing to experience the world through the sense of hearing. (2) The author would like exploring the city only to observe how people are suffering. (3) The author would spend the first day seeing the people whose kind-heartedness and friendship have enriched her life. (4) The author acknowledges that even in three days, she would not be able to see everything. (5) The author feels that everyone can find the greatest joy merely by the sense of touch. (6) The author advises those who can see to appreciate every moment as if they might lose their senses tomorrow. (7) On the third day, the author would like to observe people in their daily lives and understand their experiences.
Complete the table with what Helen Keller wanted to do on the three days if she had sight.

The author says that you need eyes to be able to 'see' the true self of a person. Therefore, she refers to the eye as 'window of the soul'. How is the narrator able to understand the feelings of a person?

Read the extracts and answer the questions. Extract 1: 'I feel the delicate symmetry of a leaf...' (i) What does the 'delicate symmetry of a leaf' symbolise? (ii) List two phrases from the extract that describe the texture of objects in nature. (iii) Complete: The phrase 'awakening Nature after her winter's sleep' refers to ________. (iv) What is the tone of the writer in this extract? A. nostalgic B. proud C. admiring D. celebratory.

Read the extracts and answer the questions. Extract 2: 'The next day I should arise with the dawn...' (i) Which word does not match with 'thrilling'? A. experience B. tale C. knowledge D. adventure. (ii) Why does the writer refer to the earth as 'sleeping earth'? (iii) The writer wishes to dedicate the day to a brief look at the present and past world because ________. (iv) Why does the writer use 'should' multiple times in the extract?

Answer the following questions: (1) The sense of touch makes up for the loss of sight and hearing. Explain with reference to the text. (2) Why does the author believe that the sense of sight is the most wonderful? (3) How might the author's opinion on making the most of our senses guide us to be kinder towards people with special abilities? (4) What is the significance of imagining the loss of a sense? (5) How does the author encourage people to approach their everyday sensory experiences? (6) What do the author's choices for the three days tell us about her values and priorities?

question_bank.stem: Work in pairs. Recall a visit to a place you liked. Discuss and write: (1) Name of the place (2) The person(s) you went with (3) Things you could: see, listen, smell, taste, touch and feel.
How does the memory of the place you visited make you feel?
Do you think your visit/experience would be different if you could not see or hear? Share your answers.

Work in pairs. Identify the statements that are true: (1) The author expresses a deep longing to experience the world through hearing. (2) The author would like exploring the city only to observe suffering. (3) The author would spend the first day seeing friends who enriched her life. (4) The author acknowledges that even in three days she could not see everything. (5) The author feels that everyone can find the greatest joy merely through touch. (6) The author advises those who can see to appreciate every moment as if they might lose their senses. (7) On the third day, the author would observe people in their daily lives.

Complete the table with what Helen Keller wanted to do on the three days if she had sight. Day 1: see the people, know her friends from the feel of their faces, … Day 2: arise with the dawn, … Day 3: [blank].
question_bank.table:
Day|What Helen Keller wanted to do
Day 1|see the people, know her friends from the feel of their faces, …
Day 2|arise with the dawn, …
Day 3|

What does the 'delicate symmetry of a leaf' symbolise?
List two phrases from the extract 'I feel the delicate symmetry of a leaf...' that describe the texture of objects in nature.
Complete the sentence: The phrase 'awakening Nature after her winter's sleep' refers to __________________.
What is the tone of the writer in the extract 'I feel the delicate symmetry of a leaf…'?
Which of the following words does not match with the word 'thrilling'?
Why does the writer refer to the earth as 'sleeping earth'?
The writer wishes to dedicate the day to a brief look at the present and past world because __________________.
Why does the writer use 'should' multiple times in the extract?
The sense of touch makes up for the loss of sight and hearing. Explain this statement with reference to the text.
Why does the author believe that the sense of sight is the most wonderful?
How might the author's opinion on making the most of our senses guide us to be kinder towards people with special abilities?
What is the significance of imagining the loss of a sense, according to the author?

tasks_verbatim: Sensory words are descriptive — they describe how we experience the world. Complete the following table with sensory words from the text in Column 2 for the five senses in Column 1. Add new words in Column 3.

Read the following sentences from the text and underline the verbs (I should divide the period... I should want to see... I should arise with the dawn... I should behold with awe...). Modal verbs are auxiliary verbs that express necessity, possibility, permission, or ability. Identify the modal verbs in the following sentences and choose the functions they express: possibility, necessity, polite request, ability, moral obligation.

Read the situations in Column 1 and functions in Column 2. Fill in the blanks in the sentences in Column 4 with appropriate modal verbs from Column 3. After filling in the blanks, explain the function of each modal verb to your classmates.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table with sensory words from the text and add new words. Senses: What you see (panorama), What you hear (music), What you smell (perfume), What you taste (relish), What you feel (rough).
question_bank.table:
Sense|From text|New words
What you see|panorama|
What you hear|music|
What you smell|perfume|
What you taste|relish|
What you feel|rough|

question_bank.stem: Identify the modal verbs in the following sentences and choose the functions they express (possibility, necessity, polite request, ability, moral obligation): (1) You can learn a lot from this experience. (2) They might arrive late due to traffic. (3) She must finish her homework before dinner. (4) They ought to apologise for their mistake. (5) He would like to have some lassi, please.

Fill in the blanks with appropriate modal verbs. Situations: (1) You want to leave work early — permission: _____ I leave early today if I finish all my tasks? (2) You have an important deadline tomorrow — obligation: I _____ finish this report by tomorrow. (3) Your friend is thinking whether to join Art class — advice: You _____ consider all the pros and cons. (4) There are dark clouds in the sky — possibility: It _____ rain later today. (5) Asking someone to pass the salt — polite request: _____ you pass the salt, please? (6) Advising someone to apologise — moral obligation: You _____ apologise for the mistake. (7) Deciding where to go for picnic — suggestion: We _____ go to any park nearby.

tasks_verbatim: You will listen to a conversation between a mother and son. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below.

question_bank.stem: Listen to a conversation between a mother and son. Select four true statements from: (1) Anuj finds the concept of Braille fascinating and wants to learn more. (2) Mother is unsure of the school's efforts to include students with visual impairment. (3) Anuj initially thinks that Digital India is only about learning how to use computers. (4) Mother believes that screen readers are very effective for persons who are visually impaired. (5) Anuj already knew that persons who are visually impaired can use computers and smartphones. (6) Anuj wants to know about the effectiveness of assistive technologies. (7) Mother believes a right mindset is the best support for persons who are visually impaired.

tasks_verbatim: Work in pairs. Take turns to speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most. Use the given prompts to frame your response before you speak. Remember to: speak clearly and confidently, glance at your notes for reference rather than reading, make eye contact, use gestures if you wish to, pronounce words clearly and avoid mumbling, speak at a steady moderate pace.

question_bank.stem: Work in pairs. Take turns to speak for a minute on the sense you value the most. Use prompts: I value my sense of sight/sound/touch/smell/taste the most because... One of my favourite experiences is... This is so because... I appreciate being able to see/hear/touch/smell/taste... God forbid, if I were to lose this sense, it would make me feel... I would not be able to... I thank God that I am able to...

tasks_verbatim: A descriptive paragraph describes a person, place, object or event to create a memorable experience for the reader. Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently. Follow the guidelines: (1) In the opening line, begin with something interesting to capture the reader's attention. Introduce the place you visited. (2) In the supporting lines, describe the place using adjectives and specific details that appealed to your five senses. You may include alliterations, similes, and personifications. (3) In the concluding lines, summarise the description. Mention what was the most important thing about that place which others should also experience.

question_bank.stem: Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place you visited recently. (1) Begin with an interesting opening line introducing the place. (2) In the supporting lines, describe the place using adjectives and details for all five senses — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — including alliterations, similes, personifications. (3) In the concluding lines, summarise and mention the most important thing about the place.

tasks_verbatim: Do you know how persons with visual challenges are able to read and write? They read through touch with the help of Braille, a code of raised dots that represents the letters of the alphabet. Study the Braille code given below and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.
Have you heard about the International Day of Persons with Disabilities? Find out when and how it is observed in India. Write your findings on a sheet and put it up on the class board.
Do you want to know about sign language? Go to the link given to learn about the Indian sign language: https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/accessibility/ISL_200word_v15.pdf

question_bank.stem: Study the Braille alphabet code and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.
Find out when and how the International Day of Persons with Disabilities is observed in India. Write your findings on a sheet and put it up on the class board.
Learn about Indian sign language using the NCERT link provided. (https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/accessibility/ISL_200word_v15.pdf)
